21.1.label: Question 21.1
21.1.scenario: A large long-term health insurance company is considering entering the health insurance market in a developing country, which has a rapidly growing economy.
21.1.question: N/A

(i).question: State the main advantages and disadvantages of each of the following methods of entering the market: (a) establishing a local branch or wholly-owned subsidiary (b) acquiring an existing local health insurance company (c) entering into a partnership with a local company to create a new jointly-owned health and care insurance company.

Merits of different methods - New branch: keeps all profits ... but finances it all
maintains/uses own brand name... but brand name means nothing in new area
may have difficulty attracting good staff due to lack of reputation
initially small - may struggle to achieve critical mass
Merits of different methods - Acquire lo: keeps all profits ... and needn't buy unless it is pretty sure the company is sound ... but it may get it wrong
may be regulatory restrictions on foreign ownership
Merits of different methods - Joint vent: doesn't meet all the cost ... but doesn't keep all the profit
quite different control issues depending on relative shares 40/60 or 50/50 or 60/40 etc
no historical baggage (products, systems) as venture is brand new
staff unless some can be seconded from local partner
new name to establish but might use name of joint venture partner

(ii).question: Describe the factors which the insurer should consider to assess the viability of establishing a wholly owned subsidiary long-term health insurance company in the developing country.

Steps to assess viability: The aim is to be able to build a flexible model office for the new subsidiary. This would be used for various purposes:
modelling the expenses and profits from writing business to see whether entering the market is worthwhile
planning the capital support required
showing a business plan to the regulator (assuming there is a regulator).
To be able to build this model a lot of research would be needed into the following areas.
The products sold in the market now
the products sold in related competing markets
any tax advantages / disadvantages of health products
any other restrictions on product design
the scope for new products to be sold (eg existing domestic designs adapted for the developing country)
Market:
the market share that might be achieved
whether the market is expanding
the competition, and any likely new entrants, and the reactions of the competition
the cost to break into the market, eg advertising and establishing sales networks
the type of operation that would need to be set up given likely sales volumes
where to base the business
the costs of rent, any building costs or purchase price of property required
the systems required
staff requirements and level of remuneration / other costs
timescales to get the business up and running
reserves and capital requirements on set up
restrictions on asset selection (eg due to local regulations)
the impact of reserving rules on releasing profit
whether profit could be recognised earlier anyway for accounting purposes, eg by embedded value techniques
how profit could be passed back to the parent company ... and any tax implications of this
the benefits of reinsurance
the type and level of reinsurance (if any) that will be appropriate
This research would lead to a tentative stab at a strategic plan. This would then be subjected to further research considering:
the customer demand for the new products
the detailed terms and conditions
the local regulator's view.
The model would be further refined. It would then be used to answer the key questions (profitability, capital requirements).
The sensitivity of profit etc to economic changes, variations in business volumes, variations in experience etc would be tested.

(iii).question: Outline the principal problems that would be encountered in practice in determining suitable assumptions for projecting the financial performance of the new subsidiary.

Principal problems setting assumptions: The insurer is unlikely to have any relevant experience itself in this country.
A partner insurer may have relevant experience, ... however the insurer should consider the reliability of the data ... and the credibility.
There may not be any relevant insurance industry standard tables available for the developing country.
Data may be available from third parties such as reinsurers or consultants but this may have a cost associated with it.
It may also be very limited if there are not existing health insurance products available in the country.
Local health data could be used, for example any information gathered by the government on the health of the population. However, this would need to be adjusted for the expected insurance population.
The insurer will also need to consider the expected future claims trends. Again, these may be difficult to determine due to a lack of available data and local market expertise.
Given these issues it is likely that the insurer would require significant margins in any morbidity and mortality assumptions to allow for the risk of setting the assumptions incorrectly.
Claim costs:
Depending on the products sold, the insurer may also need to determine claim amount assumptions. These may be difficult to determine due to a lack of experience in the market.
Data on the cost of treatments may not be readily available from providers in the country.
Lapse rates:
There may not be any relevant past data to help the insurer determine suitable lapse rates.
This will depend in part on the sales channel used. If the insurer is able to sell its products via an existing sales channel there may be some data available.
The insurer would need to use this with care though as there may be other factors affecting its lapse rates.
For example, lapses may increase in the future if other insurers enter the market.
Expenses and inflation:
The expenses the insurer will incur entering the new market are largely unknown and may be significant costs.
It will need to consider the cost of:
researching the market
determining the required method of entry
negotiating any acquisitions or mergers
developing products for market.
For any products, the insurer will need to consider the costs of the following functions: underwriting, renewals, claims.
For each function the insurer may need to: recruit staff, train staff, establish procedures, monitor compliance with procedures.
The insurer will also need to set an appropriate expense inflation assumption. This may be difficult if there is no published index for expense inflation in this country.

Even if there is a suitable index, the rate may not reflect that experience by the insurer. For example, the insurer may have to increase staff pay at a higher rate than average in order to attract and retain suitably skilled staff.

It could consider the difference in the returns from index-linked and fixed-interest government bonds as the rate of inflation. This assumes that both types of bonds are available in the developing country.
Investment return:

Depending on the product the insurer sells this may not be a significant assumption. However, it may still be difficult for the insurer to determine. There may not be an established market for assets in the country making the determination of the expected investment return difficult.
This may lead to high volatility in the expected returns which would need to be considered.
If government assets are considered, there may also be a political risk if the government is not stable, leading to increased uncertainty in returns.
Even determining the future interest rate assumptions could be difficult if the local government does not have a clear and stable economic policy.
Local firms may not offer many investments in the local currency if this is also volatile. For example, they may offer US dollar denominated bonds.
Other:
The new business volume assumptions will be another significant unknown for the insurer. The insurer may not have a clear view of the demand for the product.
This will also have an impact on its expense assumptions and how many policies the fixed overhead costs are divided between.
The new business mix is also likely to be very uncertain.
The insurer should take particular care if there are any cross-subsidies in its pricing basis.
The tax rate assumption may be difficult to determine if there is not a stable government with an established policy on this.

21.2.label: Question 21.2
21.2.scenario: A small but well-established proprietary life insurer writes a wide range of long-term life insurance business (mostly savings business) in both the group and individual market. To date it has not written any health insurance business, but it is now considering launching its own range of critical illness products.
21.2.question: N/A

(i).question: Suggest reasons why the life insurer may be considering this proposal.

Reasons why the life insurer may be cons: diversification, so that the insurer can spread its risks
follow other long-term insurers who write CI insurance
to meet increasing demand for CI insurance in recent years combined with declining demand for other life products
the demand may be coming from customers (eg employers) and also sellers
CI may be more profitable than existing products

CI can extend the product offering, for both group as well as individual markets, and so make it easier to sell each product. (For example, group CI sits well as a 'packaged product' with group life and/or pensions.)
economies of scale - eg it may not cost that much more to sell a CI product alongside a life product
desire to increase awareness of their own brand name
may be encouraged to write CI by reinsurers
more data built up on CI experience over the years, and so this small insurer now feels it can take on the uncertainties involved with this product, eg price with more certainty
to give cross-selling opportunities to current life insurance policyholders
to make efficient use of spare capital

(ii).question: Outline the points that would be made in the report in the following sections: (a) details of recommended investigations and issues to consider (b) limitations of the results of the above investigations (c) risks and issues that each area of the business will need to face if this proposal is accepted, suggesting ways in which these could be mitigated.

Investigations and other considerations: Recommend that a financial model be constructed to model the financial position of the company with and without the new product. Alternatively a model can be built for future business only.
Management will need to agree on a criterion for the assessment of the proposal.
For example, this may be based on long-term profitability or the estimated level of new business sales, depending on the company's strategy.
This model will require a number of assumptions, particularly in respect of the new product.
The main assumptions will be: new business volumes, premium levels, lapse rates, expenses, claims experience.
The impact of the new products on each assumption will need to be considered, ... in some cases, this will require some investigation.
Decide what product types are to be provided in both the individual and group markets.
For the individual market, accelerated-CI would seem the natural extension of the company's existing life products, but could also sell stand-alone CI.
Alternative product ranges could be modelled to assess their financial impact.

Similarly, need to decide what benefits and premiums to offer ... and what distribution channels to use - existing channels could be easier (assuming this is where much of the demand is coming from), ... but the mix of sales by channel may be different after the launch.
Also, consider whether premium rates (and benefits) are to be guaranteed or reviewable. This has implications for reserving and solvency capital requirements.
A key assumption will be expected sales volumes. These should be split by product type, distribution channel, etc.
Research should be carried out to assess the expected demand for the new CI products.
Investigations could be carried out to assess this by: looking at demand for similar products offered by competitors; conducting market research.
In addition, the proposed products must be able to meet the needs of the target customers.
Should also consider how these products fit in with the brand image.
Competitor research could help to determine the following features of the new product: expected profitability, level of premium rates, level of demand, benefit structure, etc.

Also consider the current: stage of the CI market cycle - eg are new CI premiums currently very high?; economic climate and outlook - ie do consumers have much disposable income and can companies afford group CI?
This will influence whether the timing of the launch is appropriate.
The insurer will require data on CI claims.
There is no internal data, and it will have to rely on external sources, such as industry-wide tables or data from reinsurers or from overseas markets.
The data used will need to be adjusted to allow for differences in benefits, target markets, etc.
Allow for the (one-off) expense of the launches... and the ongoing (ie initial, renewal and claims) expenses of the new products.
Initial and claims expenses are generally likely to be higher than comparable life products due to the additional complexity involved.

Allow for the impact of the launch on the existing business. For example, this may result in more sales of the insurer's life products if CI can be sold at the same time. (If only the new products are being modelling, this synergy will need to be explicitly allowed for.)
Consider what level and type of reinsurance will be appropriate.
Immediately following the launch, this small insurer should consider a high level of proportional reinsurance. This will depend on the level of risk the insurer is prepared to take on.
Need to be aware of, and allow for, differences in regulations and taxation for the new products.
Similarly, the taxation of products with mainly CI benefits may be different from many life products. There may be differences in the taxation of profits, premiums or benefits.
Key staff will need to be trained or recruited before the launch (see below).
Alternatively, the main specialist areas, such as underwriting and claims, could be outsourced to a third party.
The shift from savings to protection business is likely to lead to a change in investment strategy, ie to one that is more cautious and short term.
The above considerations may be different for both the individual and group markets.
Can obtain advice on many of the above matters from consultants.
The results of the modelling exercise can also help identify areas where the insurer is most at risk.
For example, the profitability of the company may be very sensitive to changes in claims experience.
The insurer can then take action to mitigate these risks - for example by changing the product design to unit-linked.
Limitations of the results: The decision whether or not to start writing CI could be based on the strategy that gives the highest present value of the future profits of the company (both new and existing business).
This measure would be simple, but there is a danger that the decision is being based on results that may be very dependent on the assumptions used.

There may be great subjectivity over the criterion that is appropriate (for example, the term to consider the future profits), ... the discount rate used and many of the other assumptions, particularly where data is scarce.
The availability and reliability of the data used is a severe limitation on the results. The data used may not be sufficient in volume to be credible.
Therefore the results of the financial model will just be a tool to aid decision making, along with other considerations (such as those outlined in the previous section).
Scenario and sensitivity testing should be carried out to assess the sensitivity of the results to certain assumptions.
These will help assess the level of confidence in the decision being made. Stochastic modelling may be appropriate in some cases.
The limitations of the results should be clearly made out to management when they are presented.
Risks and issues for specific areas (inc: Sales:
The new products will be different and more difficult to explain. Also, complying with sales regulations will be more difficult.
For example, the definitions of certain benefits (ie medical conditions) can be complex.
The risks of mis-selling are arguably greater with CI insurance.
Specific training should be provided. Commission may need to be enhanced to reflect increased time spent on sales.
Products will need to be designed carefully and sales methods monitored with this in mind.
Underwriting:
Specialist underwriters with critical illness knowledge will be required.
They will need to appreciate the impact that certain factors and medical conditions have on the risk of CI claims (rather than on the probability of dying).
Claims:
Greater, and more complex, evidence will be required to validate claims (ie not just a death certificate).
Many claims are not clear cut and there is also greater potential for fraud.
Claims assessors will need a greater understanding of medical conditions.
Pricing, reserving and financial planning (data issues):
Initially, internal data will be inadequate. Can use external data but blend this with internal data as own experience builds up.
Greater margins may therefore be required.
Systems:

Systems will need to be redesigned to allow for the new products. For example, there may be differences in: the medical data that needs to be captured; the system checks required; the reasons for claiming.
For accelerated CI products, the systems will need to cope with both death and CI claims.
Other mitigation:
Reinsurers may be able to help with many aspects - eg underwriting, claims control and pricing.
Underwriting and claims staff will require training; specialist expertise could be recruited.
Alternatively these functions could be outsourced, at least initially.

21.3.label: Question 21.3
21.3.scenario: A proprietary health insurance company (Co A) based in a developed country is interested in setting up an operation in an overseas territory which it believes has significant new business potential. Another proprietary health insurance company (Co B), based in the same country as Co A, has a subsidiary life insurance company (Co C) in this overseas territory which it has just put up for sale. Co C sells long-term health insurance business.
21.3.question: N/A

(i).question: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages to Co A of purchasing Co C rather than setting up a new operation in the overseas territory.

Advantages: Co C is already set up and in place. Co A wouldn't have to register a new company or seek authorisation to write business.
Registration may be difficult or even impossible for a foreign company.
The company would also be in place physically, with all the necessary systems set up to run the business.
Co C has a history that may prove useful. Past experience analyses will be useful for pricing reviews and a past claims acceptance record (if good) will help to sell health and care products.
The workforce is already there so there'd be no need for a massive recruitment and training exercise, which would be costly and time consuming.
The purchase price may be less than the overall cost of setting up a new operation.
If a new company were being set up from scratch, specialist knowledge would need to be sought on local regulations and tax issues as well as learning about the local health insurance market.
The distribution channels are already in place, so products could be sold immediately.
The brand name will already be known locally, which should overcome the possible issue of wariness towards unknown foreign companies.
Lack of knowledge concerning language and customs could cause delays if starting from scratch.
Disadvantages: Co A believes that the overseas territory has significant new business potential. This potential may be in areas that are not covered by Co C's range of products, so Co C may not be best placed to take advantage of this potential.
For example, Co C only sells long-term business at present, so has no market presence for PMI business (which is a problem if Co A intends to sell PMI in this overseas market).
With all this potential it may seem strange that Co B is selling Co C. This may suggest some problem of which Co A is unaware.

Assuming Co A wants to put in some of its own people to run Co C, there will be redundancies. Apart from the cost of the redundancy package, there may be ill-will created among the remaining employees.
Therefore retention bonuses may be needed to keep key staff.
Co A will inherit all the business of Co C. Some of this may be loss-making.
Some products may be out-of-date, hard to manage or have guarantees or options that Co A may find onerous.
Although the purchase price would take these into account, there is still an increased risk for Co A.
The purchase price may be higher than the cost of starting from scratch.
Starting from scratch is more flexible in that different parts of the company can be expanded as and when the finance is available, rather than having to start with a full-size company.
This slower approach can help reduce the risks associated with a new venture.
Co A will inherit the IT systems of Co C. These may be out-of-date and there may be many different systems for different types of business.
Co A will also inherit Co C's reputation and past track record for paying claims. These may not be good.
How easy will it be for Co C to continue functioning without its link to Co B? Are there any services provided by Co B that Co A will find hard to replace?

(ii).question: Outline the factors that Co A should consider before deciding whether or not to launch this new type of product.

Data: Co A will need to consider the data that is available to help price the new product. Its own data may not be relevant as it will relate to a very different product, socio-economic group and territory.

It may have relevant own data if it has any similar microinsurance products sold in other developing countries. This data would need to be adjusted for changes in demographic profiles between the countries involved.

If there is no appropriate own data, Co A could consider purchasing data from another organisation. Reinsurers may be able to help with this, as might consultants. Again, this data would need to be adjusted for changes in business mix and product design.

Any data relating to other microinsurance products may have issues regarding the amount of data available and the accuracy of the data given issues around collecting data on microinsurance policyholders eg due to low levels of literacy.
Thus this data may therefore not be viewed as credible.
External data would have a cost associated with it which would need to be allowed for in the pricing of the product.
Given these issues around access to data to help price the product, Co A may find it hard to determine an appropriate price for cover.
This may lead to low profits, or even losses being made, given microinsurance tends to have low premiums and low profit margins anyway.
Distribution: An integral part of Co A's decision about how it will distribute the product will be to decide how to launch the product. It could: launch its own product to the market; enter into a joint venture with an existing organisation in the country; buy an existing insurer.

If the insurer enters into a joint venture with an existing organisation it will need to consider: the reputation of the existing organisation; the location of the existing organisation within the country; the financial status of the existing organisation; any issues regarding investment from an overseas company in the foreign country; any issues regarding ownership and repatriation of profits from a domestic perspective eg regulatory issues, tax implications etc.

Additionally, Co A should consider any language difficulties that may exist. A joint venture may alleviate these issues, however its senior management may still be required to oversee operations and this may be difficult.
Co A will need to weigh up the costs of all these options for entering the market and decide which is the most cost effective.
The insurer may want to investigate whether there are any grants or funds available to encourage insurance products being launched in the new country.
Once it has decided how to distribute the product it may need to determine appropriate commission or payment rates for policies sold.
It will also need to consider how the premiums are going to be collected. There may be a lack of bank accounts in the country and so typical payment methods may not be appropriate.
It may be possible to use mobile phone technology to pay for premiums.
Demand: Co A will also need to consider the expected demand for its new product.
It should consider any existing microinsurance products that are available in the country and how successful these initiatives have been.
A lack of financial awareness in the country may make it difficult to sell policies.
It may need to provide financial education as part of its marketing strategy to raise awareness of the need for insurance.
Product design: The product is to be an income protection insurance product, however, it is likely to be significantly different to the insurer's standard product design.

For example: the income levels that are insured are likely to be significantly lower; benefits may be required to be paid more regularly, eg monthly; deferred periods may be zero or shorter as there may not be any other source of benefits for policyholders from the State or employers; replacement ratios may have to be higher as it may be that all of the income is being used and costs may not fall when the insured is unable to work.

Co A should also consider how this product sits with its own ethical strategy. For example, could it be viewed as taking advantage of vulnerable individuals, or would it be viewed as providing assistance to those who need it at a difficult time?
It will also need to consider how this product sits with the insurer's own risk strategy.
Underwriting and claims management: The insurer will need to consider how to underwrite applicants. It may be difficult to collect accurate data from applicants due to low levels of literacy in the population.
This lack of data has a knock-on consequence for monitoring the experience going forward and setting accurate premiums in the future.
There may also be issues getting evidence of occupations and income levels. For example, many of the population may be paid in cash on a daily basis and have no written evidence of this.

Managing claims may also be difficult for the insurer. For example: there may be transport and accessibility issues around visiting claimants; there may be a culture of resistance to outside assistance or medical intervention.
Other: Co A will need to consider a range of factors relating to the country it is considering launching the new product in, including the: political stability; economic strength; exchange rate with the domestic country and its volatility; availability of assets to back the liabilities.
It will also need to consider whether its administration systems can cope with the new product. If developments are required, the cost of these will need to be considered.
The company will also need to consider who will administer the products. If this will be done in the overseas country the insurer will need to train local staff to administer the product.
There may be issues if staff with the appropriate skill sets are not available in the country.

The insurer will need to consider the capital requirements of the product. For example: the availability of capital to develop, launch and meet solvency requirements; expected timescales for the new product to 'payback' the capital; if capital is not available, consider potential sources and the associated costs; any more efficient alternative uses of capital.
The insurer will need to consider whether, given all the considerations above, the product meets its own profitability criteria.
